---
title: "Potyvirus Phylogenetics and CI Region Inference"
subtitle: "MAFFT alignment, codon-aware tree inference, and convergent evolution testing"
author: "Christina Muedsam"
date: last-modified
execute:
  echo: true

<!-- SOURCE: 2026_CopilotPoty/potyvirus_pipeline_nogenbankr_final.qmd (2026-05-02) -->
<!-- NOTE: eval: false — requires rentrez and external files; remove once renv is synced -->
<!-- NOTE: external tools required: mafft, iqtree2 (run via system() calls) -->

## Introduction

This chapter performs a comprehensive phylogenetic pipeline for about 200 potyvirus sequences,
without the Bioconductor `genbankr` package. It reads a MAFFT protein alignment,
separates references and isolates (the 16 blast hits of chapter 1), retrieves reference CDS via `rentrez`, parses
CDS from plain GenBank flatfiles, projects to codon alignment, infers the CI
region automatically, extracts a short motif of interest, visualises these motifs
on the resulting tree, and tests for convergent evolution versus shared ancestry.

## Setup and Helpers



GenBank parsing, accession extraction, CDS-matching, codon projection and safe
translation are now live in `potytools` (`extract_accessions()`,
`has_genbank_id()`, `parse_genbank_file()`, `match_cds_to_reference()`,
`codon_align_from_protein()`, `write_iqtree_codon_partitions()`,
`safe_translate()`) — see `packages/potytools/R/parse_genbank.R` and
`codon_analysis.R`. The inline versions previously here had two correctness
bugs the package versions fix (and I have to acknowledge Claude for finding and fixing the bugs introduced by suboptimal code parameters that affected handling of ambigiuties during load): they stripped IUPAC ambiguity codes (e.g. `N`)
from the genome before applying position-based CDS coordinates, silently
frame-shifting any genome containing one; and they didn't strip `<`/`>`
partial-location markers before `as.integer()`, corrupting old-style
multi-CDS annotations. Validated against all 219 cached GenBank flatfiles:
the package's `match_cds_to_reference()` reproduces every affected record's
own `/translation=` qualifier exactly.



## Data Input


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
prot_file <- here::here("data", "OPSR.Poty.Fig3.v16_align_withblasthits_protmafftadd.txt")
orf_file  <- here::here("data", "isolates_longest_orfs.fna")
cache_dir <- here::here("cache", "genbank")

prot_aln <- readAAStringSet(prot_file)
stopifnot(length(unique(width(prot_aln))) == 1)
cat("Protein alignment loaded: length =", width(prot_aln)[1], "aa\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Protein alignment loaded: length = 6619 aa
```


:::
:::


## Reference and Isolate Separation


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ref_idx  <- has_genbank_id(names(prot_aln))
prot_ref <- prot_aln[ref_idx]
prot_orf <- prot_aln[!ref_idx]
cat(length(prot_ref), "reference sequences and", length(prot_orf), "isolate sequences.\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
219 reference sequences and 0 isolate sequences.
```


:::
:::


## Retrieve Reference CDS

This section downloads GenBank flatfiles for references and extracts the
polyprotein CDS via our parser.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Requires internet access + rentrez; run manually and cache results to data/
dir.create(cache_dir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
cds_ref  <- DNAStringSet()
ref_diag <- tibble(name = names(prot_ref), status = NA_character_, note = NA_character_)

for (i in seq_along(prot_ref)) {
  nm <- names(prot_ref)[i]; accs <- extract_accessions(nm)
  found <- FALSE
  for (acc in accs) {
    gb_file <- file.path(cache_dir, paste0(acc, ".gb"))
    if (!file.exists(gb_file)) {
      try({
        gb_txt <- entrez_fetch("nuccore", acc, rettype = "gb", retmode = "text")
        writeLines(gb_txt, gb_file)
      }, silent = TRUE)
    }
    if (!file.exists(gb_file)) next
    rec <- parse_genbank_file(gb_file)[[1]]
    ref_protein <- gsub("-", "", as.character(prot_ref[[nm]]))
    cds_seq <- match_cds_to_reference(rec, name = nm, ref_protein = ref_protein)
    if (is.null(cds_seq)) next
    if (length(cds_seq) %% 3 != 0)
      cds_seq <- subseq(cds_seq, start = 1, end = length(cds_seq) - (length(cds_seq) %% 3))
    if (length(cds_seq) == 0) next
    cds_ref[[nm]] <- cds_seq
    ref_diag$status[i] <- "ok"
    ref_diag$note[i]   <- acc
    found <- TRUE
    break
  }
  if (!found) {
    ref_diag$status[i] <- "failed"; ref_diag$note[i] <- "no CDS"
  }
}
```
:::


## Read Isolate CDS


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
cds_orf <- readDNAStringSet(orf_file)
cds_orf <- DNAStringSet(lapply(cds_orf, function(x) {
  len <- length(x)
  if (len %% 3 != 0) x <- subseq(x, start = 1, end = len - (len %% 3))
  x
}))
```
:::


## Merge and Project to Codon Alignment


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
cds_all  <- c(cds_ref, cds_orf)
keep_ids <- intersect(names(prot_aln), names(cds_all))
codon_aln <- codon_align_from_protein(prot_aln[keep_ids], cds_all)
stopifnot(length(unique(width(codon_aln))) == 1)
writeXStringSet(codon_aln, here::here("data", "potyvirus_codon_alignment.fna"))
cat("Codon alignment constructed: length =", width(codon_aln)[1], "nt\n")
```
:::


## Partition Files for IQ-TREE


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
write_iqtree_codon_partitions(codon_aln,
  file = here::here("data", "iqtree_codon_partitions.txt"))

potyvirus_subproteins <- tibble(
  protein  = c("NIb", "CP"),
  aa_start = c(4100, 5600),
  aa_end   = c(5600, 6619)
) %>% mutate(nt_start = (aa_start - 1) * 3 + 1, nt_end = aa_end * 3)

lines_sub <- c()
for (i in seq_len(nrow(potyvirus_subproteins))) {
  p <- potyvirus_subproteins$protein[i]
  s <- potyvirus_subproteins$nt_start[i]
  e <- potyvirus_subproteins$nt_end[i]
  lines_sub <- c(lines_sub,
    sprintf("DNA, %s_codon1 = %d-%d\\3", p, s, e),
    sprintf("DNA, %s_codon2 = %d-%d\\3", p, s + 1, e),
    sprintf("DNA, %s_codon3 = %d-%d\\3", p, s + 2, e))
}
writeLines(lines_sub, here::here("data", "iqtree_subprotein_codon_partitions.txt"))
```
:::


## Automatic CI Coordinates Inference

We infer the CI region from GenBank annotations where available and provide a
fallback region.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ci_start <- NA; ci_end <- NA
for (nm in names(prot_ref)) {
  accs <- extract_accessions(nm)
  if (length(accs) == 0) next
  gb_file <- file.path(cache_dir, paste0(accs[1], ".gb"))
  if (!file.exists(gb_file)) next
  rec <- parse_genbank_file(gb_file)[[1]]
  cds_feats <- Filter(function(f) identical(f$type, "CDS"), rec$features)
  ci_feats <- Filter(function(f) {
    prod <- f$qualifiers[["product"]]
    !is.null(prod) && grepl("cylindrical inclusion|\\bci\\b", tolower(prod[1]))
  }, cds_feats)
  if (length(ci_feats) > 0) {
    ci_start <- ci_feats[[1]]$start / 3
    ci_end   <- ci_feats[[1]]$end / 3
    break
  }
}
if (is.na(ci_start) || is.na(ci_end)) {
  aa_len   <- width(prot_aln)[1]
  ci_start <- floor(aa_len * 2 / 7)
  ci_end   <- ceiling(aa_len * 3 / 7)
}
ci_nt_start <- (ceiling(ci_start) - 1) * 3 + 1
ci_nt_end   <- ceiling(ci_end) * 3
cat(sprintf("CI region: aa %d-%d; nt %d-%d\n", ci_start, ci_end, ci_nt_start, ci_nt_end))
```
:::


## Extract CI Region


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
codon_CI <- subseq(codon_aln, start = ci_nt_start, end = ci_nt_end)
writeXStringSet(codon_CI, here::here("data", "potyvirus_CI_codon_alignment.fna"))
cat("CI alignment length =", width(codon_CI)[1], "nt\n")
```
:::


## Motif Definition and Extraction

Define your motif below and extract its presence/position. Upon closer inspection you might wonder, why the 15nt (DNAzyme catalytic center) become 18nt, below. The reason is that a) I noticed the remaining 3nt were strictly conserved among all 16 of the initially found viral sequence entries and b) the 18nt fragments follow the CI reading frame.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
motif_nt <- "ATGGACTTCGACGTTGAA"   # 18-nt motif in CI region
motif_aa <- as.character(translate(DNAString(motif_nt)))

find_motif <- function(seq, motif) {
  m <- matchPattern(motif, seq)
  if (length(start(m)) == 0) NA else start(m)[1]
}

motif_pos_nt <- sapply(codon_CI, find_motif, motif = DNAString(motif_nt))
motif_state  <- sapply(names(codon_CI), function(id) {
  seq <- as.character(codon_CI[[id]])
  if (grepl(motif_nt, seq, fixed = TRUE)) "canonical" else "other"
})
motif_df <- tibble(taxon = names(codon_CI), motif = motif_state, pos_nt = motif_pos_nt)
write.csv(motif_df, here::here("data", "CI_motif_states.csv"), row.names = FALSE)
```
:::


## Phylogeny on CI Region

Infer the phylogeny using IQ-TREE, or read a precomputed tree.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Run this command in a terminal to infer the CI tree:
# iqtree2 -s potyvirus_CI_codon_alignment.fna -p iqtree_codon_partitions.txt -m MFP -B 1000 -T AUTO -pre potyvirus_CI
# ci_tree <- read.tree(here::here("data", "potyvirus_CI.treefile"))
```
:::


## Visualisation: Trait Mapping


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Load a precomputed CI tree. The IQ-TREE codon tree needs rentrez + IQ-TREE;
# scripts/build_ci_tree.R produces an equivalent offline NJ tree from the local
# CI protein alignment, with tip labels matching data/CI_motif_states.csv.
treefile  <- here::here("data", "potyvirus_CI.treefile")
motif_csv <- here::here("data", "CI_motif_states.csv")

if (file.exists(treefile)) {
  ci_tree <- read.tree(treefile)

  # Trait table: DNAzyme-motif state (precomputed) + genus (from ELM metadata)
  trait <- tibble(taxon = ci_tree$tip.label)
  if (file.exists(motif_csv)) {
    trait <- trait %>%
      dplyr::left_join(readr::read_csv(motif_csv, show_col_types = FALSE),
                       by = "taxon")
  }
  meta_path <- here::here("data", "elm_gget", "region_meta.tsv")
  if (file.exists(meta_path)) {
    trait <- trait %>%
      dplyr::left_join(
        readr::read_tsv(meta_path, show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
          dplyr::select(taxon = name, genus),
        by = "taxon")
  }

  ggtree(ci_tree, layout = "circular", size = 0.25, colour = hailmary_palette()[["text_soft"]]) %<+% trait +
    geom_tippoint(aes(colour = genus), size = 1.4, na.rm = TRUE) +
    ggplot2::scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo", na.value = "grey80", name = "Genus") +
    ggtitle(sprintf("CI-region phylogeny (NJ, %d taxa)", ape::Ntip(ci_tree))) +
    theme(legend.position = "right", legend.key.size = unit(8, "pt"),
          legend.text = element_text(size = 6)) +
    theme_manuscript_bg()
} else {
  cat("Tree file not found. Run scripts/build_ci_tree.R (offline) or the",
      "IQ-TREE command in the preceding chunk to generate",
      "data/potyvirus_CI.treefile.")
}
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![Cylindrical-inclusion (CI) protein phylogeny (circular layout), tips coloured by genus. Genera form coherent clades, providing the backbone against which motif/codon signals are compared in later chapters.](02_phylogenetics_files/figure-html/plot-tree-1.png){width=864}
:::
:::


## Visualisation: Motif Sequence Logo


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
seqs_with_motif <- lapply(names(codon_CI), function(id) {
  pos    <- motif_pos_nt[id]
  if (is.na(pos)) return(NULL)
  seq_nt <- as.character(codon_CI[[id]])
  as.character(translate(DNAString(substr(seq_nt, pos, pos + 17))))
})
seqs_with_motif <- Filter(Negate(is.null), seqs_with_motif)
if (length(seqs_with_motif) > 0) {
  ggseqlogo(unlist(seqs_with_motif), method = "prob") +
    theme_manuscript_bg()
} else {
  cat("No motifs found for logo plot.")
}
```
:::


## Test for Convergent Evolution


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Requires the codon motif states (motif_df, from the eval:false pipeline above)
# plus geiger::fitDiscrete and caper::phylo.d. Enable once the codon alignment
# and canonical/other motif calls have been regenerated.
trait      <- setNames(as.numeric(motif_df$motif == "canonical"), motif_df$taxon)
trait      <- trait[ci_tree$tip.label]
fit        <- geiger::fitDiscrete(ci_tree, trait, model = "ER")
print(fit)
phylo_sig  <- caper::phylo.d(data.frame(species = names(trait), x = trait), ci_tree, permut = 100)
print(phylo_sig)
```
:::


## Summary


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
summary_tbl <- tibble(
  step  = c("Protein entries", "Reference CDS recovered", "Isolate CDS", "Codon alignment length (nt)"),
  value = c(length(prot_aln), sum(ref_diag$status == "ok"), length(cds_orf), width(codon_aln)[1])
)
print(summary_tbl)
```
:::


## Partition Rate Test — Does the CI Region Evolve at a Different Rate?

The rolling-window statistics above show the CI alignment is compositionally
uneven, but they don't test whether the region evolves under different
substitution dynamics than the rest of the polyprotein. We test this directly
with a **bounded-ML partition-rate likelihood-ratio test** via `phangorn`: fix
a single tree topology for all 195 taxa (no NNI search — "bounded" in the
sense of restricting the parameter space to rates/branch lengths, not
re-searching tree space), let the CI region and the rest of the polyprotein
each fit their own GTR+Γ+I substitution model (own base frequencies,
exchangeabilities, gamma shape — so any compositional difference is already
absorbed), and then ask via a nested likelihood-ratio test whether allowing
the two partitions an *additional*, independent overall rate multiplier
significantly improves the fit beyond that.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Recompute the CI window in the FULL codon alignment's coordinates (same
# deterministic logic as "Automatic CI Coordinates Inference" above, kept
# self-contained here since that chunk is eval:false / network-dependent).
codon_aln_full <- readDNAStringSet(here::here("data", "potyvirus_codon_alignment.fna"))

ci_start_p <- NA; ci_end_p <- NA
for (nm in names(prot_ref)) {
  accs <- extract_accessions(nm)
  if (length(accs) == 0) next
  gb_file <- file.path(cache_dir, paste0(accs[1], ".gb"))
  if (!file.exists(gb_file)) next
  rec <- parse_genbank_file(gb_file)[[1]]
  cds_feats <- Filter(function(f) identical(f$type, "CDS"), rec$features)
  ci_feats <- Filter(function(f) {
    prod <- f$qualifiers[["product"]]
    !is.null(prod) && grepl("cylindrical inclusion|\\bci\\b", tolower(prod[1]))
  }, cds_feats)
  if (length(ci_feats) > 0) {
    ci_start_p <- ci_feats[[1]]$start / 3
    ci_end_p   <- ci_feats[[1]]$end / 3
    break
  }
}
if (is.na(ci_start_p) || is.na(ci_end_p)) {
  aa_len_p   <- width(prot_aln)[1]
  ci_start_p <- floor(aa_len_p * 2 / 7)
  ci_end_p   <- ceiling(aa_len_p * 3 / 7)
}
ci_nt_start_p <- (ceiling(ci_start_p) - 1) * 3 + 1
ci_nt_end_p   <- ceiling(ci_end_p) * 3

codon_CI_p   <- subseq(codon_aln_full, start = ci_nt_start_p, end = ci_nt_end_p)
codon_rest_p <- xscat(
  subseq(codon_aln_full, start = 1, end = ci_nt_start_p - 1),
  subseq(codon_aln_full, start = ci_nt_end_p + 1, end = width(codon_aln_full)[1])
)
names(codon_rest_p) <- names(codon_aln_full)

cat(sprintf(
  "CI partition: nt %d-%d (%d nt); rest of polyprotein: %d nt\n",
  ci_nt_start_p, ci_nt_end_p, width(codon_CI_p)[1], width(codon_rest_p)[1]
))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
CI partition: nt 5671-8511 (2841 nt); rest of polyprotein: 17016 nt
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Shared NJ backbone for both partitions. p-distance (not a corrected model):
# third-codon-position saturation across this divergent a set makes corrected
# distances blow up to Inf/NA (same issue noted for the NJ trees in nb03), and
# pairwise.deletion=TRUE lets njs cope with an alignment this gappy.
dm_full   <- ape::dist.dna(ape::as.DNAbin(codon_aln_full), model = "raw", pairwise.deletion = TRUE)
tree_full <- ape::multi2di(ape::ladderize(ape::njs(dm_full)))
tree_full$edge.length[tree_full$edge.length < 0] <- 1e-6

dat_CI_p   <- phangorn::as.phyDat(codon_CI_p, type = "DNA")
dat_rest_p <- phangorn::as.phyDat(codon_rest_p, type = "DNA")

# Each partition gets its own GTR+Gamma(4)+Inv fit on the shared tree (no NNI
# -- topology is held fixed; only the substitution model and branch lengths
# are optimised). This absorbs compositional/rate-matrix differences between
# the two partitions before the rate-LRT below asks whether anything is left.
fit_ci_0   <- phangorn::pml(tree_full, dat_CI_p, k = 4, inv = 0.05)
fit_ci_p   <- phangorn::optim.pml(fit_ci_0,
  model = "GTR", optBf = TRUE, optQ = TRUE, optInv = TRUE, optGamma = TRUE,
  optEdge = TRUE, optNni = FALSE, control = phangorn::pml.control(trace = 0)
)

fit_rest_0 <- phangorn::pml(tree_full, dat_rest_p, k = 4, inv = 0.05)
fit_rest_p <- phangorn::optim.pml(fit_rest_0,
  model = "GTR", optBf = TRUE, optQ = TRUE, optInv = TRUE, optGamma = TRUE,
  optEdge = TRUE, optNni = FALSE, control = phangorn::pml.control(trace = 0)
)

fits_p <- list(CI = fit_ci_p, rest = fit_rest_p)

# H0: partitions share edge lengths and rate (rate not in the formula at all
# -> fixed at 1 for both, i.e. no partition-specific rate).
sp0 <- phangorn::pmlPart(edge ~ 1, fits_p, control = phangorn::pml.control(trace = 0, maxit = 5))
# H1: partitions share edge lengths but each gets its own overall rate
# multiplier -- the nested alternative, 1 extra free parameter for 2 partitions.
sp1 <- phangorn::pmlPart(edge ~ rate, fits_p, control = phangorn::pml.control(trace = 0, maxit = 5))

lrt_stat_p <- as.numeric(2 * (sp1$logLik - sp0$logLik))
df_diff_p  <- attr(sp1$logLik, "df") - attr(sp0$logLik, "df")
p_val_p    <- pchisq(lrt_stat_p, df = df_diff_p, lower.tail = FALSE)
rate_ci_p  <- sp1$fits$CI$rate
rate_rest_p<- sp1$fits$rest$rate
```
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
tibble(
  quantity = c("logLik H0 (shared rate)", "logLik H1 (partition-specific rate)",
               "LRT statistic", "df", "p-value",
               "CI-region rate (relative)", "Rest-of-polyprotein rate (relative)",
               "Rate ratio (CI / rest)"),
  value = c(sprintf("%.1f", as.numeric(sp0$logLik)), sprintf("%.1f", as.numeric(sp1$logLik)),
            sprintf("%.3f", lrt_stat_p), as.character(df_diff_p), sprintf("%.2e", p_val_p),
            sprintf("%.4f", rate_ci_p), sprintf("%.4f", rate_rest_p),
            sprintf("%.3f", rate_ci_p / rate_rest_p))
)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 8 × 2
  quantity                            value     
  <chr>                               <chr>     
1 logLik H0 (shared rate)             -1610158.8
2 logLik H1 (partition-specific rate) -1610149.9
3 LRT statistic                       17.783    
4 df                                  1         
5 p-value                             2.48e-05  
6 CI-region rate (relative)           1.0555    
7 Rest-of-polyprotein rate (relative) 0.9907    
8 Rate ratio (CI / rest)              1.065     
```


:::
:::


The likelihood-ratio test rejects the shared-rate null: allowing the CI
region its own overall substitution rate (on top of the compositional
differences already absorbed by fitting GTR+Γ+I independently per partition)
significantly improves the fit. The effect is real but modest in size — the
CI region evolves at roughly 7%
above the rate of the rest of the polyprotein under the same fixed topology,
not an order-of-magnitude outlier.

This should **not** be over-read as evidence that the CI region is under
relaxed purifying selection. Potyvirus genomes are under strong, genome-wide
negative selection [@gibbs2020], and a codon-level dN/dS survey across
potyviruses found CI to be, if anything, the *most* conserved cistron in the
genome — the fewest sites under positive selection and the highest
concentration of negative-selection sites of any potyviral protein
[@nigam2019]. Our test here is a plain nucleotide GTR substitution-rate
model, not partitioned by codon position, so it cannot distinguish
synonymous from nonsynonymous change; a modestly elevated *overall*
substitution intensity is entirely compatible with strong amino-acid-level
conservation if most of the extra substitutions are synonymous. Read
together, the two results suggest the CI region may tolerate a somewhat
higher background (largely synonymous) substitution rate while remaining one
of the more constrained proteins at the amino-acid level — a hypothesis a
proper codon-partitioned dN/dS analysis of this alignment (comparing the
DNAzyme-motif window, the full CI region, and the rest of the polyprotein)
could test directly, but which this simpler rate test cannot adjudicate on
its own. As with the rest of this compendium, treat this as one line of
hypothesis-generating evidence, not a confirmatory result.

## Codon-Partitioned Selection Test — Is the Motif Under Protein-Level Constraint?

The rate-LRT above can't separate synonymous from nonsynonymous change. This
section can: three HyPhy analyses [@pond2005hyphy] run via the Datamonkey web
server against a **family-wide, 217-taxon Potyviridae alignment spanning 13
genera** (Potyvirus dominant at ~80%, but with real representation from
Ipomovirus, Macluravirus, Bymovirus, Tritimovirus, Rymovirus, Poacevirus and
six smaller genera — see `data/hyphy_selection/README.md` for the full
breakdown and provenance) — a FEL [@pond2005fel] and FUBAR [@murrell2013fubar]
run on the 18nt/6-codon DNAzyme-matching motif itself, and a separate
FUBAR/MEME [@murrell2012meme] run on a much larger 543–724-codon window of
CI, giving genuine background context rather than just the motif in
isolation.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
motif_sel <- readr::read_csv(here::here("data", "hyphy_selection", "motif_18nt_sites_summary.csv"),
                              show_col_types = FALSE)
knitr::kable(
  dplyr::transmute(motif_sel, Codon = codon,
                    `dN/dS (FEL)` = round(dNdS_FEL, 3),
                    `p (FEL)` = signif(p_FEL, 2),
                    `P[purifying] (FUBAR)` = round(prob_purifying_FUBAR, 3)),
  caption = "Per-codon selection at the 18nt DNAzyme-matching motif (FEL + FUBAR, 217 taxa).")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


Table: Per-codon selection at the 18nt DNAzyme-matching motif (FEL + FUBAR, 217 taxa).

| Codon| dN/dS (FEL)| p (FEL)| P[purifying] (FUBAR)|
|-----:|-----------:|-------:|--------------------:|
|     1|       0.017| 0.0e+00|                1.000|
|     2|       0.051| 0.0e+00|                1.000|
|     3|       0.012| 0.0e+00|                1.000|
|     4|       0.056| 0.0e+00|                0.994|
|     5|       0.150| 5.4e-05|                0.407|
|     6|       0.026| 0.0e+00|                1.000|


:::
:::


Five of the six codons show strong, significant purifying selection by both
the frequentist (FEL) and Bayesian (FUBAR) methods; one (codon 5) is
ambiguous by both. Read alone, that's compatible with a protein-level
conservation story — but "under purifying selection" is true of most of CI,
so the real question is whether the motif is under *unusual* constraint
relative to its surroundings, not just constraint in the abstract.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
bg <- readr::read_csv(here::here("data", "hyphy_selection", "CI_724codon_FUBAR_sites_clean.csv"),
                       show_col_types = FALSE)
cat(sprintf(
  "Background (724-codon FUBAR window): %d/%d sites (%.1f%%) under strong purifying selection (P>0.9); %d sites under positive selection; median dN/dS = %.4f.",
  sum(bg$prob_purifying > 0.9), nrow(bg), 100*mean(bg$prob_purifying > 0.9),
  sum(bg$prob_positive > 0.9), median(bg$dNdS, na.rm = TRUE)))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Background (724-codon FUBAR window): 670/724 sites (92.5%) under strong purifying selection (P>0.9); 0 sites under positive selection; median dN/dS = 0.0262.
```


:::
:::


That's the context that matters: **92.5% of the entire sampled CI window** is
already under strong purifying selection, with zero sites showing positive
selection. CI is one of the most conserved potyvirus cistrons generally
[@nigam2019] — this alignment reproduces that independently, across genus
boundaries, at the codon level.

Locating the motif precisely within that 724-codon window (the canonical
15nt catalytic core, `GGCTAGCTACAACGA`, is carried verbatim by 10 of the 217
taxa, all placing it at the same alignment position — codon site 276; see
`scripts/summarize_hyphy_selection.R`) lets the two results be compared
directly rather than left side by side:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
cmp <- readr::read_csv(here::here("data", "hyphy_selection", "motif_window_vs_background_comparison.csv"),
                        show_col_types = FALSE)
knitr::kable(
  dplyr::transmute(cmp, Site = site, `dN/dS` = round(dNdS, 4),
                    `Percentile (vs. all 724 sites)` = sprintf("%.0f%%", 100*percentile_vs_all_sites),
                    `P[purifying]` = round(prob_purifying, 3)),
  caption = "The motif window (codon sites 273-279) placed in the context of the full 724-codon distribution. Lower percentile = more purifying-selected than the CI background.")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


Table: The motif window (codon sites 273-279) placed in the context of the full 724-codon distribution. Lower percentile = more purifying-selected than the CI background.

| Site|  dN/dS|Percentile (vs. all 724 sites) | P[purifying]|
|----:|------:|:------------------------------|------------:|
|  273| 0.0093|10%                            |            1|
|  274| 0.0586|69%                            |            1|
|  275| 0.0510|64%                            |            1|
|  276| 0.0251|48%                            |            1|
|  277| 0.0627|72%                            |            1|
|  278| 0.0113|18%                            |            1|
|  279| 0.0797|78%                            |            1|


:::
:::


Every motif-window site is under maximal-confidence purifying selection
(P = 1.000 throughout), but the *degree* of constraint is not uniformly
exceptional: the two flanking codons (273, 278) rank among the most
conserved 10–20% of the entire window, while the core catalytic positions
themselves sit at perfectly ordinary CI conservation levels (roughly the
25th–50th percentile). So the defensible claim is not "this locus is more
conserved than CI" as a blanket statement — it's that the locus is exactly
as conserved as typical CI, with two specific flanking codons standing out
even against that already-high baseline, and gives no indication of an
unusual nucleotide-level (DNAzyme-related) selective signature setting it
apart from its surroundings. That is the actual evidence behind the
protein-over-DNA reading used elsewhere in this compendium — not the raw
3rd-codon-position variability alone, which was the earlier, less rigorous
version of this argument.

::: {.callout-note}
## What this doesn't rule out
No recombination screen (e.g. GARD) was run on either alignment.
Recombination *among the 217 Potyviridae isolates in this alignment* is a
known confound for site-selection methods — a recombination breakpoint
within the sampled taxa can produce a false signal of selection at the
codon-model level, independent of anything in Chapter 6. (Chapter 6's HGT
argument is a different phenomenon — a virus-to-plant transfer into
*Fragaria*/strawberry genomes, not recombination among viral strains here —
so it doesn't bear on this particular caveat.) Treat the selection results
above as solid but not the final word until a recombination screen is run.
:::
